title: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney
authors: Laurence H. Beck, Jr., David J. Salant

items.subtopic: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney
items.title: Acute Tubulointerstitial Nephritis: Recognizing Acute Kidney Injury
items.content: Acute TIN often presents with acute kidney injury due to aggressive inflammatory infiltrates causing tissue edema, tubular cell injury, and compromised flow. Flank pain may occur from renal capsule distention.
keyPoints: Acute TIN commonly presents with acute kidney injury.
Inflammatory infiltrates lead to tissue edema, tubular cell injury, and compromised tubular flow.
Flank pain can result from renal capsule distention.
Urinary sediment may show leukocytes and cellular casts.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney

items.subtopic: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney
items.title: Chronic Tubulointerstitial Nephritis: Identifying Tubular Dysfunction and Imaging Findings
items.content: Chronic TIN manifests with disorders of tubular function, such as polyuria from nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, Fanconi's syndrome features, or type IV RTA. Renal ultrasonography may show increased echogenicity, loss of corticomedullary differentiation, and cortical scarring.
keyPoints: Chronic TIN presents with disorders like polyuria (nephrogenic diabetes insipidus), Fanconi's syndrome, or type IV RTA.
Polyuria results from impaired concentrating ability; Fanconi's syndrome includes glycosuria, phosphaturia, amino aciduria, hypokalemia, and bicarbonaturia.
Type IV RTA leads to non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis and hyperkalemia due to impaired ammoniagenesis.
Renal ultrasonography may reveal increased echogenicity, loss of corticomedullary differentiation, prominence of renal pyramids, and cortical scarring.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney

items.subtopic: ACUTE INTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS | ALLERGIC INTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS | Therapeutic agents
items.title: Identify and Discontinue Potential Offending Drugs in AIN
items.content: Acute interstitial nephritis (AIN) is often encountered as an allergic reaction to drugs. In cases of unexplained acute kidney injury, a significant proportion may be due to drug-induced AIN, though biopsy-proven cases account for only ~15%. Potentially offending medications should be identified and discontinued empirically when there's a rising serum creatinine.
keyPoints: AIN is often an allergic reaction to drugs.
~15% of unexplained acute kidney injury cases are biopsy-proven AIN, likely an underestimate.
Common offending therapeutic agents include antibiotics (e.g., β-lactams, sulfonamides), NSAIDs, diuretics, anticonvulsants, immune modulators, and others like proton pump inhibitors.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · ACUTE INTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS | ALLERGIC INTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS | Therapeutic agents | Infection | Autoimmune

items.subtopic: ACUTE INTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS | ALLERGIC INTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS | Infection
items.title: Recognize Infectious Causes of AIN
items.content: Infections can lead to acute interstitial nephritis. Bacterial infections (e.g., Streptococcus, Staphylococcus) and viral infections (e.g., EBV, CMV) are notable causes. Awareness of these infectious etiologies is crucial for diagnosis and management.
keyPoints: Infections can cause AIN.
Bacterial causes include Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, Legionella, Salmonella, Brucella, Yersinia, Corynebacterium diphtheriae.
Viral causes include EBV, CMV, hantavirus, polyomavirus, HIV.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · ACUTE INTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS | ALLERGIC INTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS | Therapeutic agents | Infection | Autoimmune

items.subtopic: Chronic Tubulointerstitial Disorders | Metabolic Disturbances | Cystic and Hereditary Disorders (see Chap. 327) | Miscellaneous
items.title: Recognizing Drug-Induced Acute Interstitial Nephritis (AIN)
items.content: Acute interstitial nephritis often presents atypically, with acute kidney injury symptoms rather than the classic triad of fever, rash, and eosinophilia. NSAIDs are a common cause, leading to proteinuria without typical signs.
keyPoints: Classic AIN presentation includes fever, rash, peripheral eosinophilia, oliguric kidney injury after 7–10 days of treatment with methicillin or another β-lactam antibiotic.
Atypical reactions are more common; NSAID-induced AIN often presents with acute kidney injury and heavy proteinuria without fever, rash, or eosinophilia.
Kidney biopsy reveals extensive interstitial and tubular infiltration of leukocytes, including eosinophils.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Chronic Tubulointerstitial Disorders | Metabolic Disturbances | Cystic and Hereditary Disorders (see Chap. 327) | Miscellaneous

items.subtopic: Chronic Tubulointerstitial Disorders | Metabolic Disturbances | Cystic and Hereditary Disorders (see Chap. 327) | Miscellaneous
items.title: Understanding the Role of Proton Pump Inhibitors in Chronic Kidney Disease
items.content: Proton pump inhibitors may be associated with incident chronic kidney disease, potentially via prolonged subclinical interstitial nephritis.
keyPoints: The association between proton pump inhibitors and chronic kidney disease may involve an intermediate step of prolonged, subclinical interstitial nephritis.
It is not clear if this association involves a direct causative mechanism or represents a marker for other underlying conditions.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Chronic Tubulointerstitial Disorders | Metabolic Disturbances | Cystic and Hereditary Disorders (see Chap. 327) | Miscellaneous

items.subtopic: Allergic Interstitial Nephritis | Absolute Indications
items.title: When to Initiate Glucocorticoid Therapy in Allergic Interstitial Nephritis
items.content: Glucocorticoid therapy is not routinely required for allergic interstitial nephritis (AIN) but can be beneficial in severe cases where kidney injury progresses despite discontinuation of the offending agent. It should be considered when there's an imminent need for dialysis or if renal function continues to deteriorate.
keyPoints: Discontinuation of the offending drug often leads to reversal of kidney injury.
Glucocorticoid therapy may accelerate recovery but is not required in most cases.
Reserve glucocorticoids for severe kidney injury with imminent dialysis or continued deterioration.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Allergic Interstitial Nephritis | Absolute Indications | Relative Indications | SJÖGREN'S SYNDROME | TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS WITH UVEITIS

items.subtopic: SJÖGREN'S SYNDROME
items.title: Key Features and Management of Sjögren's Syndrome-Related Tubulointerstitial Nephritis
items.content: Sjögren's syndrome primarily affects exocrine glands, leading to sicca symptoms. Renal involvement includes tubulointerstitial nephritis with lymphocytic infiltrate, potentially causing impaired kidney function and distal RTA. Diagnosis is supported by anti-Ro (SS-A) and anti-La (SS-B) antibodies. Treatment starts with glucocorticoids, with maintenance therapy often required.
keyPoints: Sjögren's syndrome causes systemic autoimmune damage to exocrine glands.
Renal manifestation includes tubulointerstitial nephritis with lymphocytic infiltrate.
Diagnosis supported by positive serologic testing for anti-Ro (SS-A) and anti-La (SS-B).
Treatment begins with glucocorticoids, often requiring maintenance therapy.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Allergic Interstitial Nephritis | Absolute Indications | Relative Indications | SJÖGREN'S SYNDROME | TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS WITH UVEITIS

items.subtopic: SYSTEMIC LUPUS ERYTHEMATOSUS | GRANULOMATOUS INTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS
items.title: Recognizing Predominant Tubulointerstitial Inflammation in Lupus Nephritis and Granulomatous Interstitial Nephritis
items.content: In systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), class III or IV lupus nephritis usually presents with glomerular lesions, but tubulointerstitial inflammation can predominate. This may manifest as azotemia and type IV RTA rather than typical glomerulonephritis features. In granulomatous interstitial nephritis, a chronic inflammatory infiltrate with granulomas and multinucleated giant cells is observed on biopsy. Early treatment with glucocorticoids can improve kidney function before significant fibrosis develops.
keyPoints: Tubulointerstitial inflammation in lupus nephritis may present with azotemia and type IV RTA (~50% of cases show immune complex deposits in tubular basement membranes).
Granulomatous interstitial nephritis can be chronic, relapsing, and associated with sarcoidosis-like manifestations; early glucocorticoid treatment improves outcomes.
Tuberculosis should be ruled out before treating granulomatous interstitial nephritis.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · SYSTEMIC LUPUS ERYTHEMATOSUS | GRANULOMATOUS INTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS | IgG4-RELATED SYSTEMIC DISEASE | AIN ASSOCIATED WITH THE USE OF IMMUNE CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS

items.subtopic: IgG4-RELATED SYSTEMIC DISEASE | AIN ASSOCIATED WITH THE USE OF IMMUNE CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS
items.title: Approach to IgG4-Related Systemic Disease and Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Associated AIN
items.content: IgG4-related systemic disease presents with a dense inflammatory infiltrate of IgG4-expressing plasma cells, often forming pseudotumors due to fibrosis. It can mimic other conditions like Sjögren's syndrome and is treated effectively with glucocorticoids. Immune checkpoint inhibitors increase the risk of autoimmune phenomena affecting the kidneys in 2-5% of cases, typically within 15 weeks post-therapy initiation. Management includes corticosteroids, discontinuation of inciting medications, and avoiding further doses until kidney recovery.
keyPoints: IgG4-related disease involves IgG4-expressing plasma cells; glucocorticoids are effective once diagnosed.
Immune checkpoint inhibitors can cause AIN in 2% (monotherapy) to 5% (dual therapy); acute kidney injury typically arises within 15 weeks of starting therapy.
Management of immune checkpoint inhibitor-associated AIN includes corticosteroids and discontinuation of inciting medications.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · SYSTEMIC LUPUS ERYTHEMATOSUS | GRANULOMATOUS INTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS | IgG4-RELATED SYSTEMIC DISEASE | AIN ASSOCIATED WITH THE USE OF IMMUNE CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS

items.subtopic: IDIOPATHIC AIN
items.title: Autoimmune Mechanisms in Idiopathic AIN
items.content: Idiopathic Acute Interstitial Nephritis (AIN) may involve an autoimmune response, as evidenced by the presence of autoantibodies targeting tubular antigens. This condition is responsive to glucocorticoid therapy but can have a relapsing course.
keyPoints: Autoantibodies target collecting duct or proximal tubular brush border antigens
Responsive to glucocorticoid therapy
May require maintenance treatment with another immunosuppressive agent
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · IDIOPATHIC AIN | INFECTION-ASSOCIATED AIN | CRYSTAL DEPOSITION DISORDERS AND OBSTRUCTIVE TUBULOPATHIES

items.subtopic: INFECTION-ASSOCIATED AIN
items.title: Distinguishing Infection-Associated AIN from Acute Bacterial Pyelonephritis
items.content: Infection-associated AIN, often seen in immunocompromised patients like kidney transplant recipients with polyomavirus BK reactivation, should be differentiated from acute bacterial pyelonephritis. The latter does not generally cause acute kidney injury unless it affects both kidneys or leads to septic shock.
keyPoints: Common in immunocompromised patients
Polyomavirus BK reactivation is a notable cause
Acute bacterial pyelonephritis rarely causes AKI unless bilateral or with septic shock
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · IDIOPATHIC AIN | INFECTION-ASSOCIATED AIN | CRYSTAL DEPOSITION DISORDERS AND OBSTRUCTIVE TUBULOPATHIES

items.subtopic: CRYSTAL DEPOSITION DISORDERS AND OBSTRUCTIVE TUBULOPATHIES
items.title: Management of Acute Kidney Injury from Crystal Deposition
items.content: Acute kidney injury can result from crystal deposition in tubules, leading to obstruction. Management includes saline volume repletion and drug withdrawal for reversible cases, while emergent treatment with hemodialysis or rasburicase is required for severe urate nephropathy.
keyPoints: Crystals can cause AKI by depositing in tubular cells
Saline volume repletion and drug withdrawal are initial treatments
Hemodialysis or rasburicase needed for severe urate nephropathy
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · IDIOPATHIC AIN | INFECTION-ASSOCIATED AIN | CRYSTAL DEPOSITION DISORDERS AND OBSTRUCTIVE TUBULOPATHIES

items.subtopic: LIGHT CHAIN CAST NEPHROPATHY | Light Chain Cast Nephropathy
items.title: Diagnosis and Clinical Clues in Light Chain Cast Nephropathy
items.content: Light chain cast nephropathy (LCCN) should be suspected in patients with acute kidney injury unresponsive to correction of precipitating factors, especially in the context of multiple myeloma or monoclonal gammopathy. Key clinical clues include anemia, bone pain, hypercalcemia, and a narrow anion gap due to hypoalbuminemia and hypergammaglobulinemia.
keyPoints: Consider LCCN when acute kidney injury persists after correction of precipitating factors or in elderly patients with unexplained acute kidney injury.
Clinical clues: Anemia, bone pain, hypercalcemia, narrow anion gap due to hypoalbuminemia and hypergammaglobulinemia.
Urinary dipsticks detect albumin but not light chains; negative dipstick results with increased protein in urine suggest Bence-Jones proteins.
Use serum and urine protein electrophoresis and immunofixation for detecting monoclonal bands.
Sensitive methods are available to detect free light chains in urine and serum.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · LIGHT CHAIN CAST NEPHROPATHY | Light Chain Cast Nephropathy

items.subtopic: LIGHT CHAIN CAST NEPHROPATHY | Light Chain Cast Nephropathy
items.title: Treatment Goals and Considerations in Light Chain Cast Nephropathy
items.content: The primary treatment goals for light chain cast nephropathy include correcting precipitating factors like hypovolemia and hypercalcemia, discontinuing nephrotoxic agents, and addressing the underlying plasma cell dyscrasia. The role of plasmapheresis in removing light chains is uncertain.
keyPoints: Correct precipitating factors such as hypovolemia and hypercalcemia.
Discontinue potential nephrotoxic agents like NSAIDs or radiographic contrast media.
Treat the underlying plasma cell dyscrasia to manage LCCN effectively.
Plasmapheresis for removing light chains has questionable value in treating LCCN.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · LIGHT CHAIN CAST NEPHROPATHY | Light Chain Cast Nephropathy

items.subtopic: LYMPHOMATOUS INFILTRATION OF THE KIDNEY | CHRONIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL DISEASES | VESICOURETERAL REFLUX AND REFLUX NEPHROPATHY
items.title: Lymphomatous Infiltration of the Kidney: Recognize and Manage Oliguric Acute Kidney Injury
items.content: Lymphomatous infiltration is often found incidentally in patients with chronic lymphocytic leukemia or non-Hodgkin's lymphoma. Rarely, it can cause significant kidney enlargement and oliguric acute kidney injury. Management involves high-dose glucocorticoids and chemotherapy, but prognosis remains poor.
keyPoints: Common autopsy finding in chronic lymphocytic leukemia and non-Hodgkin's lymphoma
Rarely causes massive kidney enlargement and oliguric acute kidney injury
Treatment: High-dose glucocorticoids and chemotherapy
Prognosis is generally poor
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · LYMPHOMATOUS INFILTRATION OF THE KIDNEY | CHRONIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL DISEASES | VESICOURETERAL REFLUX AND REFLUX NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: LYMPHOMATOUS INFILTRATION OF THE KIDNEY | CHRONIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL DISEASES | VESICOURETERAL REFLUX AND REFLUX NEPHROPATHY
items.title: Reflux Nephropathy: Early Detection and Management to Prevent ESRD
items.content: Reflux nephropathy results from vesicoureteral reflux in childhood, leading to interstitial scarring and tubular atrophy. It often goes unnoticed until adulthood when chronic kidney disease is detected. Management includes monitoring for hypertension, proteinuria, and kidney injury. Early detection and management are crucial to prevent progression to end-stage renal disease.
keyPoints: Caused by vesicoureteral reflux or urologic anomalies in childhood
Results in patchy interstitial scarring and tubular atrophy
Often undetected until adulthood; may present with hypertension, proteinuria, mild kidney injury
Ultrasound shows asymmetric small kidneys with irregular outlines and thinned cortices
Early detection and management can prevent progression to ESRD
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · LYMPHOMATOUS INFILTRATION OF THE KIDNEY | CHRONIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL DISEASES | VESICOURETERAL REFLUX AND REFLUX NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: Vesicoureteral Reflux and Reflux Nephropathy
items.title: Management of Vesicoureteral Reflux to Prevent Kidney Damage
items.content: Surgical reimplantation of the ureters is indicated for young children with persistent high-grade reflux but not effective in adolescents or adults after scarring. Aggressive control of blood pressure and glomerular hyperfiltration using ACEIs, ARBs, or SGLT2 inhibitors can reduce proteinuria and forestall kidney function deterioration.
keyPoints: Surgical reimplantation is indicated for young children with high-grade reflux but not in adolescents/adults after scarring.
ACEIs, ARBs, and SGLT2 inhibitors are effective in reducing proteinuria and preserving kidney function.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Vesicoureteral Reflux and Reflux Nephropathy | SICKLE CELL NEPHROPATHY | TUBULOINTERSTITIAL ABNORMALITIES ASSOCIATED WITH GLOMERULONEPHRITIS

items.subtopic: SICKLE CELL NEPHROPATHY
items.title: Early Detection and Management of Sickle Cell Nephropathy
items.content: Subtle renal abnormalities such as polyuria or type IV RTA may appear in childhood due to sickle cell nephropathy. Early recognition, including microalbuminuria, warrants nephrology consultation and possibly low-dose ACEI therapy. Papillary necrosis can cause gross hematuria and ureteric obstruction.
keyPoints: Polyuria or type IV RTA may indicate early tubular injury in sickle cell disease.
Microalbuminuria suggests the need for nephrology consultation and possible low-dose ACEI therapy.
Papillary necrosis can lead to gross hematuria and ureteric obstruction.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Vesicoureteral Reflux and Reflux Nephropathy | SICKLE CELL NEPHROPATHY | TUBULOINTERSTITIAL ABNORMALITIES ASSOCIATED WITH GLOMERULONEPHRITIS

items.subtopic: ANALGESIC NEPHROPATHY | ARISTOLOCHIC ACID NEPHROPATHY
items.title: Analgesic Nephropathy: Recognizing and Managing Long-term Analgesic Use Complications
items.content: Analgesic nephropathy arises from prolonged use of compound analgesics containing phenacetin, aspirin, and caffeine. It is characterized by impaired kidney function due to papillary necrosis caused by drug concentration in the inner medulla. Radiographic findings include small, scarred kidneys with papillary calcifications. Patients may experience polyuria and non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis from tubular damage. A significant risk includes increased incidence of urothelial malignancy in those with end-stage renal disease (ESRD) due to analgesic nephropathy.
keyPoints: Long-term use of compound analgesics containing phenacetin, aspirin, and caffeine
Characteristic findings: impaired kidney function, papillary necrosis, small scarred kidneys with papillary calcifications
Clinical manifestations: polyuria, non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis, gross hematuria, ureteric colic
Increased risk of urothelial malignancy in ESRD patients due to analgesic nephropathy
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · ANALGESIC NEPHROPATHY | ARISTOLOCHIC ACID NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: ANALGESIC NEPHROPATHY | ARISTOLOCHIC ACID NEPHROPATHY
items.title: Aristolochic Acid Nephropathy: Identifying and Diagnosing Exposure-Related Kidney Damage
items.content: Aristolochic acid nephropathy (AAN) results from exposure to aristolochic acid, found in certain traditional herbal remedies. It is linked to Chinese herbal nephropathy and Balkan endemic nephropathy. AAN leads to renal interstitial fibrosis with minimal cellular infiltrates and a bland urine sediment. Diagnosis requires two of three features: characteristic histology on kidney biopsy, confirmation of aristolochic acid ingestion, or detection of aristolactam-DNA adducts in tissue. Due to its mutagenic effects, AAN is associated with a high incidence of upper urinary tract urothelial cancers.
keyPoints: Aristolochic acid exposure from traditional herbal remedies
Clinical features: renal interstitial fibrosis, bland urine sediment, severe anemia relative to kidney dysfunction
Diagnostic criteria: characteristic histology, confirmed ingestion, detection of aristolactam-DNA adducts
High risk of upper urinary tract urothelial cancers; surveillance with CT, ureteroscopy, and urine cytology recommended
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · ANALGESIC NEPHROPATHY | ARISTOLOCHIC ACID NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: LITHIUM-ASSOCIATED NEPHROPATHY | TREATMENT
items.title: Monitoring and Adjusting Lithium Levels to Prevent Nephropathy Progression
items.content: In patients taking lithium, regular monitoring of kidney function is crucial. This includes frequent checks of lithium levels to avoid toxic concentrations, which are ideally kept below 1 meq/L. As kidney function declines, lithium clearance becomes less effective, necessitating careful dose adjustments.
keyPoints: Regular monitoring of kidney function in patients on lithium is essential.
Frequent checking of lithium levels to prevent toxicity; target <1 meq/L.
Adjust lithium dosing as kidney function declines due to reduced clearance.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · LITHIUM-ASSOCIATED NEPHROPATHY | TREATMENT

items.subtopic: LITHIUM-ASSOCIATED NEPHROPATHY | TREATMENT
items.title: Role of Amiloride in Managing Lithium-Induced Nephrogenic Diabetes Insipidus
items.content: Amiloride can be used to prevent and treat lithium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus by inhibiting lithium entry through the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC). However, its effectiveness in preventing chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis (CIN) remains unclear.
keyPoints: Amiloride prevents/treats lithium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus by blocking ENaC.
Unclear if amiloride can prevent lithium-induced CIN.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · LITHIUM-ASSOCIATED NEPHROPATHY | TREATMENT

items.subtopic: CALCINEURIN INHIBITOR NEPHROTOXICITY | HEAVY METAL (LEAD) NEPHROPATHY
items.title: Understanding Calcineurin Inhibitor Nephrotoxicity and Management Strategies
items.content: Calcineurin inhibitors like cyclosporine and tacrolimus can cause acute and chronic kidney injury. Acute forms may result from vascular issues or toxic tubulopathy, while chronic injury is marked by a slow reduction in glomerular filtration rate, mild proteinuria, arterial hypertension, and hyperkalemia due to tubular resistance to aldosterone. Histologic changes include interstitial fibrosis, tubular atrophy, hyalinosis of intrarenal vasculature, and focal glomerulosclerosis. Dose reduction or avoidance can mitigate chronic damage but may increase rejection risk.
keyPoints: Acute injury from vascular causes or toxic tubulopathy
Chronic injury: slow reduction in GFR, mild proteinuria, arterial hypertension, hyperkalemia
Histologic changes: interstitial fibrosis, tubular atrophy, hyalinosis, focal glomerulosclerosis
Dose adjustment may mitigate damage but increase rejection risk
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · CALCINEURIN INHIBITOR NEPHROTOXICITY | HEAVY METAL (LEAD) NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: CALCINEURIN INHIBITOR NEPHROTOXICITY | HEAVY METAL (LEAD) NEPHROPATHY
items.title: Recognizing and Addressing Lead Nephrotoxicity
items.content: Chronic lead exposure can cause tubulointerstitial nephropathy, primarily affecting the proximal tubules. Early signs include hyperuricemia due to decreased urate secretion, leading to 'saturnine gout,' hypertension, and impaired kidney function. Diagnosis is challenging; preferred methods are urinary lead measurement post-chelation or bone radiographic fluoroscopy. Chronic low-level exposure is linked to reduced kidney function, and chelation therapy can slow functional decline in patients with chronic interstitial nephritis (CIN) and high lead burden.
keyPoints: Chronic lead exposure affects proximal tubules
Early signs: hyperuricemia ('saturnine gout'), hypertension, impaired kidney function
Diagnosis via urinary lead post-chelation or bone fluoroscopy
Chelation therapy can slow decline in CIN with high lead burden
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · CALCINEURIN INHIBITOR NEPHROTOXICITY | HEAVY METAL (LEAD) NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: METABOLIC DISORDERS | CHRONIC URIC ACID NEPHROPATHY
items.title: Hyperuricemia as an Independent Risk Factor for CKD
items.content: Emerging evidence suggests that hyperuricemia is independently associated with the development of chronic kidney disease (CKD), potentially through mechanisms involving endothelial damage. This association highlights the importance of monitoring and managing uric acid levels to prevent CKD progression.
keyPoints: Hyperuricemia is an independent risk factor for CKD.
Potential mechanism: Endothelial damage.
Complex interactions between hyperuricemia, hypertension, and kidney failure are not fully understood.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · METABOLIC DISORDERS | CHRONIC URIC ACID NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: METABOLIC DISORDERS | CHRONIC URIC ACID NEPHROPATHY
items.title: Clinical Presentation and Management of Gouty Nephropathy
items.content: Gouty nephropathy, characterized by uric acid and monosodium urate crystal deposits in the kidney, leads to chronic kidney disease through intrarenal obstruction and inflammatory responses. Despite morphologic changes, early glomerular filtration rate may remain near normal. Management includes allopurinol and urine alkalinization, though these measures may not always prevent CKD progression.
keyPoints: Histological features: Crystalline deposits of uric acid and monosodium urate salts.
Inflammatory response: Lymphocytic infiltration, foreign-body giant cell reaction, fibrosis in medullary and papillary regions.
Clinical course: Insidious CKD with near-normal GFR early on despite morphologic changes.
Management: Allopurinol and urine alkalinization; effectiveness varies.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · METABOLIC DISORDERS | CHRONIC URIC ACID NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: HYPERCALCEMIC NEPHROPATHY | HYPOKALEMIC NEPHROPATHY | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE
items.title: Hypercalcemia and Kidney Injury: Mechanisms and Management
items.content: Chronic hypercalcemia, often seen in conditions like primary hyperparathyroidism or multiple myeloma, leads to tubulointerstitial disease. The earliest renal lesions include degenerative changes in epithelia of collecting ducts, distal tubules, and loops of Henle. This progresses to nephron obstruction, calcium salt precipitation, and interstitial fibrosis. Clinically, hypercalcemia impairs urine concentration due to reduced responsiveness to arginine vasopressin and defective sodium and chloride transport. Treatment focuses on reducing serum calcium levels and addressing the underlying cause.
keyPoints: Chronic hypercalcemia causes tubulointerstitial disease and kidney injury.
Lesions begin with degenerative changes in renal epithelia, particularly in collecting ducts, distal tubules, and loops of Henle.
Hypercalcemia impairs urine concentration due to reduced arginine vasopressin responsiveness and defective sodium/chloride transport.
Abdominal x-rays may show nephrocalcinosis and nephrolithiasis.
Treatment involves reducing serum calcium levels and correcting the primary abnormality of calcium metabolism.
Acute kidney injury from hypercalcemia can be reversible, but chronic dysfunction may persist even after correction.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · HYPERCALCEMIC NEPHROPATHY | HYPOKALEMIC NEPHROPATHY | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: HYPERCALCEMIC NEPHROPATHY | HYPOKALEMIC NEPHROPATHY | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE
items.title: Hypokalemia and Reversible Tubular Lesions: Prevention of Chronic Kidney Disease
items.content: Prolonged severe hypokalemia, often due to laxative or diuretic abuse, primary aldosteronism, or vomiting, can lead to reversible tubular lesions. These include vacuolar degeneration of proximal and distal tubular cells, progressing to atrophy and cystic dilation with interstitial fibrosis if uncorrected. Timely correction of hypokalemia is crucial to prevent irreversible chronic kidney disease.
keyPoints: Prolonged severe hypokalemia can cause reversible vacuolar degeneration in tubular cells.
Untreated, it progresses to tubular atrophy and cystic dilation with interstitial fibrosis.
Timely correction of hypokalemia prevents further progression to chronic kidney disease.
Persistent hypokalemia may lead to end-stage renal disease (ESRD).
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · HYPERCALCEMIC NEPHROPATHY | HYPOKALEMIC NEPHROPATHY | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney
items.question: Which clinical feature is most commonly associated with acute tubulointerstitial nephritis (TIN)?
items.explanation: Acute TIN most often presents with acute kidney injury.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney

items.subtopic: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney
items.question: What is a common finding in urinary sediment for patients with acute tubulointerstitial nephritis?
items.explanation: Urinary sediment in acute TIN is often active with leukocytes and cellular casts.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney

items.subtopic: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney
items.question: Which condition may result from defective proximal tubular reabsorption in chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis?
items.explanation: Defective proximal tubular reabsorption in chronic TIN can lead to Fanconi's syndrome.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney

items.question: Which of the following is a common cause of acute interstitial nephritis (AIN) today?
options: An allergic reaction to a drug
A postinfectious complication of scarlet fever
items.explanation: AIN is more often encountered as an allergic reaction to a drug today.

items.question: Which of the following therapeutic agents is NOT commonly associated with acute interstitial nephritis?
items.explanation: Thiazide diuretics are rarely associated with AIN.

items.question: Which autoimmune syndrome is listed as a cause of immune-mediated acute interstitial nephritis?
options: Tubulointerstitial disease related to checkpoint inhibitors
items.explanation: All listed options are autoimmune syndromes associated with immune-mediated AIN.

items.question: Which of the following agents is associated with chronic interstitial nephritis in agricultural communities?
items.explanation: Chronic exposure to heavy metals like lead and cadmium is linked with chronic interstitial nephritis in agricultural communities.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Chronic Tubulointerstitial Disorders | Metabolic Disturbances | Cystic and Hereditary Disorders (see Chap. 327) | Miscellaneous

items.subtopic: Chronic Tubulointerstitial Disorders | Metabolic Disturbances | Cystic and Hereditary Disorders (see Chap. 327) | Miscellaneous
items.question: Which metabolic disturbance can lead to nephrocalcinosis?
items.explanation: Hypercalcemia is associated with the development of nephrocalcinosis.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Chronic Tubulointerstitial Disorders | Metabolic Disturbances | Cystic and Hereditary Disorders (see Chap. 327) | Miscellaneous

items.subtopic: Chronic Tubulointerstitial Disorders | Metabolic Disturbances | Cystic and Hereditary Disorders (see Chap. 327) | Miscellaneous
items.question: What is a common clinical feature in patients with atypical NSAID-induced acute interstitial nephritis?
options: Acute kidney injury with heavy proteinuria
Rapid-onset renal failure upon drug reintroduction
items.explanation: Atypical NSAID-induced acute interstitial nephritis often presents with acute kidney injury and heavy proteinuria.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Chronic Tubulointerstitial Disorders | Metabolic Disturbances | Cystic and Hereditary Disorders (see Chap. 327) | Miscellaneous

items.question: Which of the following is an absolute indication for glucocorticoid therapy in tubulointerstitial nephritis?
options: Drug-induced acute interstitial nephritis with rapid progression of renal failure
Tubulointerstitial nephritis with uveitis (TINU) in children
items.explanation: Sjögren's syndrome is listed as an absolute indication for glucocorticoid therapy.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Allergic Interstitial Nephritis | Absolute Indications | Relative Indications | SJÖGREN'S SYNDROME | TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS WITH UVEITIS

items.question: Which of the following is a hallmark feature of Tubulointerstitial nephritis with uveitis (TINU)?
options: Lymphocyte-predominant interstitial nephritis and painful anterior uveitis
Positive serologic testing for anti-Ro (SS-A) antibodies
items.explanation: TINU is characterized by lymphocyte-predominant interstitial nephritis and painful anterior uveitis.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Allergic Interstitial Nephritis | Absolute Indications | Relative Indications | SJÖGREN'S SYNDROME | TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS WITH UVEITIS

items.question: Which of the following statements is true regarding Sjögren's syndrome and kidney involvement?
options: The renal manifestation includes distal RTA and nephrogenic diabetes insipidus.
Serologic testing for anti-La (SS-B) antibodies is usually negative.
items.explanation: Sjögren's syndrome can involve the kidneys with distal RTA and nephrogenic diabetes insipidus.

items.question: In which condition is a dense inflammatory infiltrate containing IgG4-expressing plasma cells observed?
options: AIN Associated with the Use of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors
items.explanation: A dense inflammatory infiltrate containing IgG4-expressing plasma cells is characteristic of IgG4-related systemic disease.

items.question: Which condition is associated with a protracted and relapsing course of AIN, often presenting with granulomas?
options: AIN Associated with the Use of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors
items.explanation: Granulomatous interstitial nephritis can present with a protracted and relapsing course, often including granulomas.

items.question: What is the typical time frame for an acute rise in serum creatinine after starting immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy?
items.explanation: An acute rise in serum creatinine is typically noted within 15 weeks after starting immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy.

items.subtopic: IDIOPATHIC AIN | INFECTION-ASSOCIATED AIN | CRYSTAL DEPOSITION DISORDERS AND OBSTRUCTIVE TUBULOPATHIES
items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of idiopathic acute interstitial nephritis (AIN)?
options: Presence of autoantibodies to tubular antigens in some cases.
Exclusively seen in kidney transplant recipients.
items.explanation: Idiopathic AIN can involve autoantibodies targeting tubular antigens, suggesting an autoimmune component.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · IDIOPATHIC AIN | INFECTION-ASSOCIATED AIN | CRYSTAL DEPOSITION DISORDERS AND OBSTRUCTIVE TUBULOPATHIES

items.subtopic: IDIOPATHIC AIN | INFECTION-ASSOCIATED AIN | CRYSTAL DEPOSITION DISORDERS AND OBSTRUCTIVE TUBULOPATHIES
items.question: What is a common cause of infection-associated acute interstitial nephritis in kidney transplant recipients?
items.explanation: Infection-associated AIN in kidney transplant recipients is often due to reactivation of polyomavirus BK.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · IDIOPATHIC AIN | INFECTION-ASSOCIATED AIN | CRYSTAL DEPOSITION DISORDERS AND OBSTRUCTIVE TUBULOPATHIES

items.subtopic: IDIOPATHIC AIN | INFECTION-ASSOCIATED AIN | CRYSTAL DEPOSITION DISORDERS AND OBSTRUCTIVE TUBULOPATHIES
items.question: Which drug is associated with the formation of 'sheaf of wheat' crystals in urine?
items.explanation: 'Sheaf of wheat' sulfonamide crystals are associated with sulfatidazine treatment for toxoplasmosis.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · IDIOPATHIC AIN | INFECTION-ASSOCIATED AIN | CRYSTAL DEPOSITION DISORDERS AND OBSTRUCTIVE TUBULOPATHIES

items.subtopic: IDIOPATHIC AIN | INFECTION-ASSOCIATED AIN | CRYSTAL DEPOSITION DISORDERS AND OBSTRUCTIVE TUBULOPATHIES
items.question: Which condition is a serious complication of oral Phosphosoda use?
items.explanation: Oral Phosphosoda can lead to acute phosphate nephropathy due to calcium phosphate crystal deposition.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · IDIOPATHIC AIN | INFECTION-ASSOCIATED AIN | CRYSTAL DEPOSITION DISORDERS AND OBSTRUCTIVE TUBULOPATHIES

items.subtopic: LIGHT CHAIN CAST NEPHROPATHY | Light Chain Cast Nephropathy
items.question: Which of the following factors can precipitate acute kidney injury in patients with multiple myeloma leading to light chain cast nephropathy?
options: Hypovolemia, infection, hypercalcemia, NSAIDs, radiographic contrast media
Diabetes mellitus, hypertension, chronic alcohol use
Obstructive uropathy, urinary tract infections, nephrolithiasis
items.explanation: These factors are known precipitants of acute kidney injury in the context of multiple myeloma leading to light chain cast nephropathy.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · LIGHT CHAIN CAST NEPHROPATHY | Light Chain Cast Nephropathy

items.subtopic: LIGHT CHAIN CAST NEPHROPATHY | Light Chain Cast Nephropathy
items.question: What is a common clinical clue that suggests the diagnosis of light chain cast nephropathy?
options: Hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, elevated creatinine kinase
Anemia, bone pain, hypercalcemia, abnormally narrow anion gap
items.explanation: These clinical clues are indicative of light chain cast nephropathy in patients with multiple myeloma.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · LIGHT CHAIN CAST NEPHROPATHY | Light Chain Cast Nephropathy

items.subtopic: LIGHT CHAIN CAST NEPHROPATHY | Light Chain Cast Nephropathy
items.question: Which diagnostic test is highly suggestive of the presence of Bence-Jones protein in urine?
options: Urinary dipstick showing increased albumin with a negative result for light chains
Serum creatinine and blood urea nitrogen levels
items.explanation: A urinary dipstick detecting albumin but not immunoglobulin light chains, with increased protein in a spot urine specimen, suggests Bence-Jones protein.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · LIGHT CHAIN CAST NEPHROPATHY | Light Chain Cast Nephropathy

items.question: What is a common finding at autopsy in patients with chronic lymphocytic leukemia and non-Hodgkin's lymphoma?
options: Interstitial infiltration by malignant B lymphocytes
items.explanation: Interstitial infiltration by malignant B lymphocytes is a common autopsy finding in these patients.

items.question: What has led to a decline in chronic interstitial nephritis (CIN) incidence from heavy metal exposure?
options: Improved occupational and public health measures
items.explanation: Improved occupational and public health measures have led to the decline.

items.question: What is a characteristic finding on kidney ultrasound in adults with reflux nephropathy?
options: Asymmetric small kidneys with irregular outlines and thinned cortices
items.explanation: Kidney ultrasound shows asymmetric small kidneys with irregular outlines and thinned cortices.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · LYMPHOMATOUS INFILTRATION OF THE KIDNEY | CHRONIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL DISEASES | VESICOURETERAL REFLUX AND REFLUX NEPHROPATHY

items.question: Which intervention is indicated in young children with persistent high-grade vesicoureteral reflux?
options: Surgical reimplantation of the ureters into the bladder
items.explanation: Surgical reimplantation is indicated for young children with persistent high-grade reflux.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Vesicoureteral Reflux and Reflux Nephropathy | SICKLE CELL NEPHROPATHY | TUBULOINTERSTITIAL ABNORMALITIES ASSOCIATED WITH GLOMERULONEPHRITIS

items.subtopic: Vesicoureteral Reflux and Reflux Nephropathy | SICKLE CELL NEPHROPATHY | TUBULOINTERSTITIAL ABNORMALITIES ASSOCIATED WITH GLOMERULONEPHRITIS
items.question: What is the effect of ACE inhibitors or ARBs in managing vesicoureteral reflux nephropathy?
options: They reduce proteinuria and may slow kidney function deterioration.
They are used to restore ureteral competency.
They have no significant impact on the disease progression.
items.explanation: ACE inhibitors or ARBs help reduce proteinuria and may slow further kidney function deterioration.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Vesicoureteral Reflux and Reflux Nephropathy | SICKLE CELL NEPHROPATHY | TUBULOINTERSTITIAL ABNORMALITIES ASSOCIATED WITH GLOMERULONEPHRITIS

items.question: In sickle cell nephropathy, what may present with gross hematuria?
options: Polyuria due to decreased concentrating ability
items.explanation: Papillary necrosis may result from ischemia and present with gross hematuria.

items.subtopic: ANALGESIC NEPHROPATHY | ARISTOLOCHIC ACID NEPHROPATHY
items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of analgesic nephropathy?
options: Papillary necrosis due to drug concentration in the inner medulla
items.explanation: Analgesic nephropathy is characterized by papillary necrosis due to the concentration of drugs in the inner medulla.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · ANALGESIC NEPHROPATHY | ARISTOLOCHIC ACID NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: ANALGESIC NEPHROPATHY | ARISTOLOCHIC ACID NEPHROPATHY
items.question: What is a common consequence of aristolochic acid nephropathy (AAN) related to cancer risk?
options: High incidence of upper urinary tract urothelial cancers
Higher likelihood of developing pancreatic cancer
items.explanation: AAN is associated with a very high incidence of upper urinary tract urothelial cancers.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · ANALGESIC NEPHROPATHY | ARISTOLOCHIC ACID NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: ANALGESIC NEPHROPATHY | ARISTOLOCHIC ACID NEPHROPATHY
items.question: Which diagnostic feature is NOT required for definitive diagnosis of AAN?
options: Detection of aristolactam-DNA adducts in tissue
items.explanation: Non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis is not a diagnostic feature for AAN; it's associated with analgesic nephropathy.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · ANALGESIC NEPHROPATHY | ARISTOLOCHIC ACID NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: LITHIUM-ASSOCIATED NEPHROPATHY | TREATMENT
items.question: Which of the following findings on kidney biopsy are characteristic of lithium-associated nephropathy?
options: Interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy out of proportion to glomerulosclerosis or vascular disease, small cysts or dilation of distal tubule and collecting duct
Marked lymphocytic infiltrate with significant interstitial edema
Diffuse mesangial proliferation with immune complex deposition
items.explanation: These findings are characteristic of lithium-associated nephropathy.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · LITHIUM-ASSOCIATED NEPHROPATHY | TREATMENT

items.subtopic: LITHIUM-ASSOCIATED NEPHROPATHY | TREATMENT
items.question: What is the most common renal sequela associated with the use of lithium salts?
items.explanation: The most common renal sequela of lithium use is nephrogenic diabetes insipidus.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · LITHIUM-ASSOCIATED NEPHROPATHY | TREATMENT

items.subtopic: LITHIUM-ASSOCIATED NEPHROPATHY | TREATMENT
items.question: What medication has been effective in preventing and treating lithium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus?
items.explanation: Amiloride inhibits lithium entry via ENaC and is effective in preventing and treating nephrogenic diabetes insipidus.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · LITHIUM-ASSOCIATED NEPHROPATHY | TREATMENT

items.subtopic: CALCINEURIN INHIBITOR NEPHROTOXICITY | HEAVY METAL (LEAD) NEPHROPATHY
items.question: Which of the following is a common complication of calcineurin inhibitor-induced kidney injury?
items.explanation: Hyperkalemia is a relatively common complication caused by tubular resistance to aldosterone.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · CALCINEURIN INHIBITOR NEPHROTOXICITY | HEAVY METAL (LEAD) NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: CALCINEURIN INHIBITOR NEPHROTOXICITY | HEAVY METAL (LEAD) NEPHROPATHY
items.question: What histologic change is often seen in kidney tissue affected by chronic calcineurin inhibitor use?
options: Patchy interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy
items.explanation: Histologic changes include patchy interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy, often in a 'striped' pattern.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · CALCINEURIN INHIBITOR NEPHROTOXICITY | HEAVY METAL (LEAD) NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: CALCINEURIN INHIBITOR NEPHROTOXICITY | HEAVY METAL (LEAD) NEPHROPATHY
items.question: Which condition is associated with chronic low-level lead exposure and decreased kidney function?
options: Hyperuricemia due to increased urate secretion
Hypouricemia due to increased urate secretion
Hyperuricemia due to diminished urate secretion
Hypouricemia due to diminished urate secretion
items.explanation: Chronic lead intoxication leads to hyperuricemia as a result of diminished urate secretion.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · CALCINEURIN INHIBITOR NEPHROTOXICITY | HEAVY METAL (LEAD) NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: METABOLIC DISORDERS | CHRONIC URIC ACID NEPHROPATHY
items.question: Which of the following is a distinctive histological feature of gouty nephropathy?
options: Crystalline deposits of uric acid and monosodium urate salts in the kidney parenchyma
items.explanation: Gouty nephropathy is characterized by crystalline deposits of uric acid and monosodium urate salts in the kidney parenchyma.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · METABOLIC DISORDERS | CHRONIC URIC ACID NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: METABOLIC DISORDERS | CHRONIC URIC ACID NEPHROPATHY
items.question: What condition should be distinguished from gouty nephropathy due to its association with mutations in uromodulin (UMOD)?
items.explanation: Juvenile hyperuricemic nephropathy is associated with mutations in uromodulin (UMOD) and should be distinguished from gouty nephropathy.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · METABOLIC DISORDERS | CHRONIC URIC ACID NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: METABOLIC DISORDERS | CHRONIC URIC ACID NEPHROPATHY
items.question: Which treatment has not been consistently shown to improve kidney function in asymptomatic hyperuricemia?
items.explanation: The use of allopurinol in asymptomatic hyperuricemia has not been consistently shown to improve kidney function.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · METABOLIC DISORDERS | CHRONIC URIC ACID NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: HYPERCALCEMIC NEPHROPATHY | HYPOKALEMIC NEPHROPATHY | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE
items.question: Which of the following is an early lesion in hypercalcemic nephropathy?
options: Focal degenerative change in renal epithelia
Vacuolar degeneration of proximal tubular cells
items.explanation: The earliest lesion is a focal degenerative change in renal epithelia.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · HYPERCALCEMIC NEPHROPATHY | HYPOKALEMIC NEPHROPATHY | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: HYPERCALCEMIC NEPHROPATHY | HYPOKALEMIC NEPHROPATHY | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE
items.question: What clinical defect is most strikingly observed in hypercalcemic nephropathy?
items.explanation: The most striking defect is an inability to maximally concentrate the urine.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · HYPERCALCEMIC NEPHROPATHY | HYPOKALEMIC NEPHROPATHY | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE

items.question: Which condition may lead to reversible tubular lesions characterized by vacuolar degeneration?
items.explanation: Prolonged and severe hypokalemia can lead to reversible tubular lesions.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · HYPERCALCEMIC NEPHROPATHY | HYPOKALEMIC NEPHROPATHY | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney
items.statement: Chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis often presents with nephrotic-range albuminuria.
items.explanation: Chronic TIN usually has modest proteinuria, rarely >2 g/d; however, nephrotic-range albuminuria may occur in some conditions due to secondary FSGS.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney

items.subtopic: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney
items.statement: Renal ultrasonography in chronic TIN typically shows increased echogenicity and loss of corticomedullary differentiation.
items.explanation: Chronic TIN may reveal changes such as increased echogenicity and loss of corticomedullary differentiation on renal ultrasonography.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney

items.explanation: AIN diagnosis can be missed as offending medications are often discontinued without a biopsy.

items.statement: Sjögren's syndrome is associated with positive serologic testing for anti-Ro (SS-A) and anti-La (SS-B) antibodies.

items.statement: Acute bacterial pyelonephritis generally causes acute kidney injury.
items.explanation: Acute bacterial pyelonephritis does not usually cause acute kidney injury unless it affects both kidneys or leads to septic shock.

items.statement: Prophylactic allopurinol is beneficial once tumor lysis syndrome has occurred.
items.explanation: Allopurinol is used prophylactically to reduce uric acid levels but is not effective after tumor lysis has begun.

items.statement: Light chain cast nephropathy can occur even in the absence of frank myeloma.
items.explanation: The disorder should be considered in patients with monoclonal gammopathy without frank myeloma.

items.statement: Plasmapheresis is a highly effective treatment for light chain cast nephropathy.
items.explanation: The value of plasmapheresis in treating LCCN is questionable.

items.statement: Reflux nephropathy is primarily caused by chronic pyelonephritis.
items.explanation: Reflux nephropathy results from vesicoureteral reflux, not chronic pyelonephritis.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · LYMPHOMATOUS INFILTRATION OF THE KIDNEY | CHRONIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL DISEASES | VESICOURETERAL REFLUX AND REFLUX NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: LYMPHOMATOUS INFILTRATION OF THE KIDNEY | CHRONIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL DISEASES | VESICOURETERAL REFLUX AND REFLUX NEPHROPATHY
items.statement: Reflux nephropathy often goes unnoticed until early adulthood.
items.explanation: It is frequently detected during routine evaluation or pregnancy in early adulthood.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · LYMPHOMATOUS INFILTRATION OF THE KIDNEY | CHRONIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL DISEASES | VESICOURETERAL REFLUX AND REFLUX NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: Vesicoureteral Reflux and Reflux Nephropathy | SICKLE CELL NEPHROPATHY | TUBULOINTERSTITIAL ABNORMALITIES ASSOCIATED WITH GLOMERULONEPHRITIS
items.statement: Surgical reimplantation of the ureters is effective in adolescents or adults after scarring has occurred.
items.explanation: Surgical reimplantation is ineffective and not indicated in adolescents or adults after scarring.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Vesicoureteral Reflux and Reflux Nephropathy | SICKLE CELL NEPHROPATHY | TUBULOINTERSTITIAL ABNORMALITIES ASSOCIATED WITH GLOMERULONEPHRITIS

items.subtopic: Vesicoureteral Reflux and Reflux Nephropathy | SICKLE CELL NEPHROPATHY | TUBULOINTERSTITIAL ABNORMALITIES ASSOCIATED WITH GLOMERULONEPHRITIS
items.statement: Early recognition of renal abnormalities in sickle cell disease may warrant nephrologist consultation.
items.explanation: Early recognition of subtle renal abnormalities or microalbuminuria may lead to nephrologist consultation.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Vesicoureteral Reflux and Reflux Nephropathy | SICKLE CELL NEPHROPATHY | TUBULOINTERSTITIAL ABNORMALITIES ASSOCIATED WITH GLOMERULONEPHRITIS

items.subtopic: ANALGESIC NEPHROPATHY | ARISTOLOCHIC ACID NEPHROPATHY
items.statement: Aristolochic acid is a known carcinogen from the plant Aristolochia.
items.explanation: Aristolochic acid, derived from Aristolochia species, is identified as a carcinogen.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · ANALGESIC NEPHROPATHY | ARISTOLOCHIC ACID NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: ANALGESIC NEPHROPATHY | ARISTOLOCHIC ACID NEPHROPATHY
items.statement: Analgesic nephropathy primarily causes hyperkalemia.
items.explanation: Analgesic nephropathy is characterized by impaired kidney function and non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis, not primarily hyperkalemia.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · ANALGESIC NEPHROPATHY | ARISTOLOCHIC ACID NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: LITHIUM-ASSOCIATED NEPHROPATHY | TREATMENT
items.statement: Chronic interstitial nephritis due to lithium use typically occurs after less than 5 years of treatment.
items.explanation: Chronic interstitial nephritis is more likely to occur after prolonged (>10–20 years) lithium use.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · LITHIUM-ASSOCIATED NEPHROPATHY | TREATMENT

items.subtopic: LITHIUM-ASSOCIATED NEPHROPATHY | TREATMENT
items.statement: Monitoring and adjusting lithium levels can help avoid further deterioration of kidney function.
items.explanation: Frequent monitoring and dose adjustment are crucial to prevent toxic lithium levels, especially as renal clearance declines.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · LITHIUM-ASSOCIATED NEPHROPATHY | TREATMENT

items.subtopic: CALCINEURIN INHIBITOR NEPHROTOXICITY | HEAVY METAL (LEAD) NEPHROPATHY
items.statement: Lead exposure is no longer commonly diagnosed due to its widespread use in commercial products.
items.explanation: Heavy metal nephropathy from lead is less common because of the removal of lead from most commercial products.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · CALCINEURIN INHIBITOR NEPHROTOXICITY | HEAVY METAL (LEAD) NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: CALCINEURIN INHIBITOR NEPHROTOXICITY | HEAVY METAL (LEAD) NEPHROPATHY
items.statement: Occupational exposure to cadmium can occur in workers involved in the manufacture of electrical equipment.
items.explanation: Cadmium exposure is possible for those working in manufacturing or destruction of batteries, removal of lead paint, or manufacture of alloys and electrical equipment.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · CALCINEURIN INHIBITOR NEPHROTOXICITY | HEAVY METAL (LEAD) NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: METABOLIC DISORDERS | CHRONIC URIC ACID NEPHROPATHY
items.statement: Gouty nephropathy is most likely encountered in patients with severe tophaceous gout and prolonged hyperuricemia from a hereditary disorder of purine metabolism.
items.explanation: Gouty nephropathy is indeed most commonly seen in patients with severe tophaceous gout and prolonged hyperuricemia due to a hereditary disorder.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · METABOLIC DISORDERS | CHRONIC URIC ACID NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: METABOLIC DISORDERS | CHRONIC URIC ACID NEPHROPATHY
items.statement: Allopurinol is effective in preventing uric acid nephrolithiasis and the consequences of recurrent kidney stones.
items.explanation: Treatment with allopurinol and urine alkalinization is generally effective in preventing uric acid nephrolithiasis.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · METABOLIC DISORDERS | CHRONIC URIC ACID NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: HYPERCALCEMIC NEPHROPATHY | HYPOKALEMIC NEPHROPATHY | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE
items.statement: Acute kidney injury from acute hypercalcemia may be completely reversible.
items.explanation: Acute kidney injury due to acute hypercalcemia can be reversed.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · HYPERCALCEMIC NEPHROPATHY | HYPOKALEMIC NEPHROPATHY | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: HYPERCALCEMIC NEPHROPATHY | HYPOKALEMIC NEPHROPATHY | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE
items.statement: Persistent hypokalemia cannot lead to end-stage renal disease.
items.explanation: Persistent hypokalemia can cause ESRD.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · HYPERCALCEMIC NEPHROPATHY | HYPOKALEMIC NEPHROPATHY | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney
items.assertion: Diagnosis of chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis is often challenging due to nonspecific histopathology.
items.reason: Biopsy specimens in chronic TIN rarely provide a specific diagnosis, necessitating reliance on history and imaging studies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why diagnosing chronic TIN is challenging.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney

items.subtopic: SYSTEMIC LUPUS ERYTHEMATOSUS | GRANULOMATOUS INTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS | IgG4-RELATED SYSTEMIC DISEASE | AIN ASSOCIATED WITH THE USE OF IMMUNE CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS
items.assertion: Glucocorticoids are used as first-line treatment in IgG4-related systemic disease.
items.reason: IgG4-expressing plasma cells are involved in the inflammatory process of this syndrome.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the presence of IgG4-expressing plasma cells justifies the use of glucocorticoids.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · SYSTEMIC LUPUS ERYTHEMATOSUS | GRANULOMATOUS INTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS | IgG4-RELATED SYSTEMIC DISEASE | AIN ASSOCIATED WITH THE USE OF IMMUNE CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS

items.subtopic: IDIOPATHIC AIN | INFECTION-ASSOCIATED AIN | CRYSTAL DEPOSITION DISORDERS AND OBSTRUCTIVE TUBULOPATHIES
items.assertion: Acute kidney injury from crystal deposition is reversible with saline volume repletion and drug withdrawal.
items.reason: Hypovolemia precipitates the adverse effect of crystal-induced acute kidney injury.
items.explanation: The assertion and reason are true, and hypovolemia indeed contributes to the reversibility of crystal-induced AKI with appropriate treatment.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · IDIOPATHIC AIN | INFECTION-ASSOCIATED AIN | CRYSTAL DEPOSITION DISORDERS AND OBSTRUCTIVE TUBULOPATHIES

items.subtopic: IDIOPATHIC AIN | INFECTION-ASSOCIATED AIN | CRYSTAL DEPOSITION DISORDERS AND OBSTRUCTIVE TUBULOPATHIES
items.assertion: Allopurinol is effective in treating acute urate nephropathy once oliguria has developed.
items.reason: Alkaline diuresis can increase tubular flow and solubility of uric acid.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; allopurinol is not effective after oliguria, though alkaline diuresis may help.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · IDIOPATHIC AIN | INFECTION-ASSOCIATED AIN | CRYSTAL DEPOSITION DISORDERS AND OBSTRUCTIVE TUBULOPATHIES

items.subtopic: LYMPHOMATOUS INFILTRATION OF THE KIDNEY | CHRONIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL DISEASES | VESICOURETERAL REFLUX AND REFLUX NEPHROPATHY
items.assertion: Reflux nephropathy can progress to end-stage renal disease (ESRD).
items.reason: This progression occurs despite the absence of ongoing urinary infections or reflux.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · LYMPHOMATOUS INFILTRATION OF THE KIDNEY | CHRONIC TUBULOINTERSTITIAL DISEASES | VESICOURETERAL REFLUX AND REFLUX NEPHROPATHY

items.subtopic: Vesicoureteral Reflux and Reflux Nephropathy | SICKLE CELL NEPHROPATHY | TUBULOINTERSTITIAL ABNORMALITIES ASSOCIATED WITH GLOMERULONEPHRITIS
items.assertion: Proteinuria can lead to tubulointerstitial injury in glomerular disease.
items.reason: Proteinuria-mediated damage to epithelial cells and activation of tubular cells by cytokines contribute to this process.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · Vesicoureteral Reflux and Reflux Nephropathy | SICKLE CELL NEPHROPATHY | TUBULOINTERSTITIAL ABNORMALITIES ASSOCIATED WITH GLOMERULONEPHRITIS

items.subtopic: HYPERCALCEMIC NEPHROPATHY | HYPOKALEMIC NEPHROPATHY | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE
items.assertion: Chronic hypercalcemia may lead to severe tubulointerstitial damage.
items.reason: Uncontrolled hypercalcemia results in progressive kidney injury due to nephron obstruction and interstitial fibrosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney · HYPERCALCEMIC NEPHROPATHY | HYPOKALEMIC NEPHROPATHY | GLOBAL PERSPECTIVE
